root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

the algebraic theory II: connections

Table of contents

  1. Connections and Lie brackets

Prerequisites

In this post, we continue to assume that our manifolds are embedded in an ambient euclidean space, in the style of Guillemin and Pollack’s book.

Connections and Lie brackets

Given a manifold $M$ embedded in $\bbr^r$, the standard euclidean inner product $\ang{-,-}$ on $\bbr^r$ restricts to an inner product $\ang{-,-}_p$ on each tangent space $T_p(M)$. This inner product “globalizes” to yield the metric tensor on $M$, which is the map

\begin{equation} \ang{-,-} : \calx(M) \otimes_{C^\infty(M)} \calx(M) \to C^\infty(M), \quad (X,Y) \mapsto \ang{X,Y}, \end{equation}

with

\begin{equation}\notag \ang{X,Y}(p) = \ang{X_p,Y_p} \end{equation}

for each $p\in M$. An alternate and frequently used notation for $\ang{X,Y}$ is $g(X,Y)$.

The ambient inner product also enables us to define the orthogonal complement of the tangent space $T_p(M)$ by setting

\begin{equation}\notag \nu_p(M) = \left\{ w\in \bbr^r : \ang{v,w} =0, \ \forall w\in T_p(M)\right\}. \end{equation}

We then have the fundamental direct sum decomposition

\begin{equation}\notag \bbr^r = T_p(M) \oplus \nu_p(M), \end{equation}

which yields a natural surjective linear map $\bbr^r \to T_p(M)$ which projects a vector $v\in \bbr^r$ to its (unique!) component in $T_p(M)$. We shall denote this latter projection by $v|_{T_p(M)}$.

We now have all the notation needed to define one of the fundamental objects in differential geometry. In the following definition, we interpret a vector field $X$ on $M$ (embedded in $\bbr^r$) as a smooth map $X: M \to \bbr^r$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag X_p \defeq X(p) \in T_p(M) \end{equation}

for all $p\in M$.

Definition. Let $M$ be a smooth manifold embedded in $\bbr^r$. The metric connection on $M$ (or the Levi-Civita connection) is the $\bbr$-bilinear function

\begin{equation}\notag \nabla: \calx(M) \otimes_\bbr \calx(M) \to \calx(M), \quad (X,Y) \mapsto \nabla_XY, \end{equation}

given by

\begin{equation}\notag (\nabla_XY)_p = Y_{\ast,p}(X_p)|_{T_p(M)}, \end{equation}

for $p\in M$. (Recall that the “restriction” $|_{T_p(M)}$ actually denotes the projection to the tangent space; see the discussion above.)

I mentioned that the metric connection is actually $\bbr$-bilinear without proof. In fact, the metric connection has several other important properties which are listed in the next theorem. I suggest that you prove them.

Theorem on the Properties of the Metric Connection. Let $M$ be a smooth manifold embedded in $\bbr^r$ with metric connection $\nabla$.

  1. The metric connection is actually $\bbr$-bilinear.
  2. The metric connection is $C^\infty(M)$-linear in the first argument, i.e., \begin{equation}\notag \nabla_{fX}Y = f\nabla_XY \end{equation} for all $X,Y\in \calx(M)$ and all $f\in C^\infty(M)$.
  3. The metric connection is a “derivation” in the second argument, i.e., \begin{equation}\notag \nabla_X(fY) = X(f)Y + f \nabla_XY \end{equation} for all $X,Y\in \calx(M)$ and all $f\in C^\infty(M)$.

The vector field $\nabla_XY$ is computed at a point $p\in M$ by first computing the directional derivative of $Y$ in the direction of $X_p$; then, this directional derivative is “projected” to the manifold $M$, and thus $\nabla_XY$ is exactly the component of this directional derivative that the manifold $M$ “sees.”

It is instructive to study the metric connection for some simple manifolds. For example, let’s suppose that we want to understand the vanishing condition $\nabla_XY=0$ in the case that $X$ and $Y$ are vector fields on the unit circle $M=S^1$. We may parametrize an open neighborhood of a point $p\in S^1$ via the map

\begin{equation}\notag \theta \mapsto (\cos{\theta},\sin{\theta}), \end{equation}

suitably restricted to an open interval of $\theta$’s. In the local coordinate $\theta$, the vector fields $X$ and $Y$ take the form

\begin{equation}\notag X_\theta = f(\theta)(-\sin{\theta},\cos{\theta}) \quad \text{and} \quad Y_\theta = g(\theta)(-\sin{\theta},\cos{\theta}), \end{equation}

for some unique pair of smooth functions $f(\theta)$ and $g(\theta)$. But then

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{dY_\theta}{d\theta} = g’(\theta)(-\sin{\theta},\cos{\theta}) - g(\theta)(\cos{\theta},\sin{\theta}), \end{equation}

so that if $p$ has local coordinate $\theta$, we compute

\begin{equation}\notag (\nabla_XY)_p = f(\theta)g’(\theta)(-\sin{\theta},\cos{\theta}). \end{equation}

Therefore, as long as the vector field $X$ is never zero, the condition $\nabla_XY=0$ implies that $Y$ is proportional to the uniform vector field $\theta \mapsto (-\cos{\theta},\sin{\theta})$. More geometrically, if we view $Y$ as section of the trivial vector bundle $S^1\times \bbr$ via the isomorphism $\alpha$ described above, then the vanishing condition $\nabla_XY=0$ means that $Y$ is a horizontal section.

Definition. Let $M$ be a smooth manifold embedded in $\bbr^r$. The Lie bracket on $M$ is the $\bbr$-bilinear function

\begin{equation}\notag [-,-]: \calx(M) \otimes_\bbr \calx(M) \to \calx(M), \quad (X,Y) \mapsto [X,Y] \end{equation}

given by

\begin{equation}\notag [X,Y]_p = X_{\ast,p}(Y_p) - Y_{\ast,p}(X_p) \end{equation}

for $p\in M$.

Blah!

Theorem on the Levi-Civita Connection. Let $M$ be a smooth manifold embedded in $\bbr^r$ with Levi-Civita connection $\nabla$, and let $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ be smooth vectors fields on $M$.

  1. There is an equation \begin{equation}\notag \ang{Y,Z}_{\ast,p}(v) = \ang{Y_{\ast,p}(v),Z_p} + \ang{Y_p, Z_{\ast,p}(v)} \end{equation} for all points $p\in M$ and all tangent vectors $v\in T_p(M)$.
  2. The connection is compatible with the metric, in the sense that \begin{equation}\notag X \ang{Y,Z} = \ang{\nabla_XY,Z} + \ang{Y,\nabla_XZ}. \end{equation}
  3. The connection is torsion free (or symmetric), in the sense that \begin{equation}\notag \nabla_X Y - \nabla_YX = [X,Y]. \end{equation}

Blah!
